Decision Criteria: Prior DEA proceeding history; Domain 42 Section 2 (regulatory capture) extends their existing framing; need to resubmit because 2024 participation notices were voided
Commitment Pathway: Near-certain filer regardless — goal is to get democratic exclusion framing into their participation notice; offer submission guide immediately on any positive reply
Notes: DPA filed in 2023–2024 comment period; highest DEA access of any reform org; 350K subscriber network for downstream amplification

Decision Criteria: Prior DEA proceeding history (47-page comment in 2023; filed participation notice in 2024 but was excluded); regulatory capture framing in Section 2.3 is precisely NORML's existing argument
Commitment Pathway: Near-certain filer; primary goal is Domain 42 Section 2 entering their testimony; secondary goal: NORML can validate the 2024 exclusion as itself evidence of regulatory capture — mention this in the outreach
Notes: NORML deputy director has publicly criticized DEA's selection pattern as biased; this organization does not need convincing to file — they need analytical framing support

Contact Person / Entry Point: nationaloffice@aclu.org routed to Criminal Law Reform Project
Decision Criteria: Internal authorization for new federal proceedings is the decision gate; ACLU has standing before federal agencies; Domain 42 Section 3 (disenfranchisement feedback loop) is directly within their current litigation lane
Commitment Pathway: Not a foregone filer — requires internal authorization; highest-impact uncertain outcome; if they request submission guide it is a strong positive signal; offer to walk through filing procedure; frame ask as 'this framing is absent from the anticipated participant pool'
Notes: ACLU filing on disenfranchisement grounds is analytically unique — no other anticipated participant will make this argument; 1.7M member network for downstream reach

Decision Criteria: Domain 42 already cites their own data (Locked Out 2022; Voting Rights in the Era of Mass Incarceration) — lowest cold-briefing friction of any contact; activation barrier is demonstrating that Domain 42 extends their existing framework
Commitment Pathway: Research staff recognize the argument immediately as a synthesis of their published work; specific ask is bounded and concrete; offer to connect them with ACLU contact if coordination is useful
Notes: Their own data is already in the domain — they are not receiving an unfamiliar argument; easiest of the four Tier 1 contacts to convert to COM

Decision Criteria: Smaller organization with limited federal proceeding capacity; law enforcement voice is unusually credible in DEA proceedings; Section 2.4 (four scenario framework) and democratic accountability failure argument is directly in LEAP's rhetorical lane
Commitment Pathway: Treat as high-upside contact; primary ask: file a short participation notice; secondary ask: if they cannot file independently can they refer Domain 42 to NORML or DPA federal policy contacts
Notes: LEAP's credibility with DEA is disproportionate to its size; a LEAP participation notice framing democratic accountability failure carries different institutional weight than a civil rights org filing the same argument

Decision Criteria: Advocacy staff are engaged with the issue; best realistic outcome is referral to larger coalition partners rather than independent filing; Section 3 (felony disenfranchisement and student population) is the direct entry point
Commitment Pathway: Primary role: amplifier and referral node; ask them to share Domain 42 through chapter network; ask if they have working relationships with DPA or NORML federal policy staff
Notes: Network of 200+ campus chapters; even without filing, SSDP sharing Domain 42 generates downstream contacts who may engage DPA or NORML

Contact Person / Entry Point: naacpldf.org/contact webform — Policy and Advocacy team
Decision Criteria: Longer internal approval process than DPA/NORML/ACLU — 7–10 business days minimum; Domain 42 Section 3.2 (4.5% Black voting-eligible adults disenfranchised) and Section 3.3 (NYC felony arrest disparity data) are the entry points; LDF mandate is voting rights and democratic participation
Commitment Pathway: Frame as a voting rights and democratic participation issue — not drug policy; lead with Section 3 not Section 2; if ACLU is reviewing the briefing by May 10 mention this explicitly; their internal authorization path benefits from coalition signal
Notes: Tier 2 despite high impact because of routing lag; do not push past May 10 — window becomes unworkable

Decision Criteria: Domain 42 already cites PPI's felony disenfranchisement research and their 2026 Winnable Criminal Justice Reforms report; same low-friction dynamic as Sentencing Project; best realistic outcome is research amplification and potential co-filing with Sentencing Project
Commitment Pathway: Reach research team; frame as 'your data is already in the domain — you should know this analysis exists'; ask whether they plan to publish on the DEA-1362 hearing; hearing participation is secondary ask
Notes: High downstream value even without filing — PPI publishing on DEA-1362 using Domain 42 Section 3 framing is a success metric

Decision Criteria: Primarily a state-level organization; federal hearing participation is possible but not their primary lane; Section 4 (SAFER Banking and 280E analysis) and Section 4.2 (eight-introduction-cycle failure) are the relevant entry points
Commitment Pathway: Forward to MPP federal policy director; primary value is as referral node to their federal policy contacts and congressional allies rather than as independent filer; note May 24 electronic deadline explicitly in subject line
Notes: Best positioned to pass Domain 42 to congressional contacts working on SAFER Banking — secondary distribution value exceeds direct filing value

Contact Person / Entry Point: mason.marks@yale.edu — confirm current institutional email before sending
Decision Criteria: Methodological peer contact; Domain 42 Section 2.3 cites his Yale Law Journal analysis (Separation of Drug Scheduling Powers) and his February 2026 Regulatory Review piece; most likely of all contacts to provide testimony of unique analytical value
Commitment Pathway: Lead with academic framing not advocacy; ask whether the domain's regulatory capture analysis extends his Yale Law Journal argument correctly; if he is planning to testify Domain 42's democratic exclusion framing complements his statutory analysis; two specific methodological questions to pose
Notes: Verify current institutional email before sending — may have moved from Yale Law; his testimony if selected would be highest-quality contribution to the administrative record among all contacts

Organization: Ohio State Moritz College of Law — DEPC
Decision Criteria: Administrative law expertise; Domain 42 Section 6.1 (independent ALJ pool reform proposal) is the analytical ask — is the APA procedural challenge to DEA ALJ structure viable?
Commitment Pathway: Lead with the administrative law procedural question not advocacy; ask whether the independent ALJ pool argument holds as an APA matter; lower probability of DEA filing than Category A/B contacts; value is methodological validation and possible academic testimony
Notes: Lower priority than Mason Marks; most valuable as validation source for the Section 6.1 APA argument; useful even without hearing participation

Decision Criteria: Largest recreational market; Prop 64 (2016 57.13%) is the most-cited state democratic will example; 280E exposure ($2.24B estimated annual excess taxes) is the sharpest financial harm to document; SAFER Banking letter signatory
Commitment Pathway: Do not ask to file from scratch — ask whether existing DEA-1362 comment being prepared through SAFER Banking coalition can incorporate Domain 42 Section 4 federalism analysis; frame as: Domain 42 provides the democratic design context for the federalism argument your office has already made
Notes: AG offices route slowly; May 14 send gives 10 calendar days to May 24; realistic outcome at Stage 3 is 'awareness and potential incorporation' not new filing; highest-value if they already have a comment in preparation

Decision Criteria: First recreational legalization state (Amendment 64 2012 55.32%); Section 4.3 (federalism dimension) directly applies; Colorado AG has been active on cannabis banking issues
Commitment Pathway: Same as California AG — ask about incorporating federalism argument into existing DEA-1362 work; note Amendment 64's vote margin as specific democratic legitimacy evidence
Notes: Second priority among state AGs after California; useful if SAFER Banking coalition is already coordinating AG participation in the hearing

Decision Criteria: Michigan Proposal 18-1 (2018 56%); third-largest recreational market; active state-federal coordination on cannabis policy
Notes: Third priority among state AGs; include if capacity allows on May 14 send

Contact Person / Entry Point: atg.wa.gov/contact — VERIFY current AG before sending
Decision Criteria: One of first two recreational legalization states (I-502 2012 55.7%); Bob Ferguson (former AG) is now Governor — must verify current AG and their cannabis policy position before send
Commitment Pathway: Same ask; note specifically that Bob Ferguson's transition to Governor may have changed the office's cannabis coalition coordination — verify before sending
Notes: Lowest priority among state AGs; contact verification is mandatory before send — wrong contact wastes the window; if current AG is unknown by May 14 skip rather than send to lapsed contact
